findings.description: renderAccountPage() caches personalized PII under a key that omits user identity and returns cache hits without verifying ownership, leaking one user's account data to any other user requesting the same path+query.
findings.reasoning: cacheKey() is built from path and query only; userId is never incorporated. On a cache hit, renderAccountPage() returns the stored object immediately (`if (hit) return hit;`) with no comparison between the cached page's userId and the requesting userId. The userId argument is used only on the cache-miss branch, so it gates the data-fetch but not the data-return — making the per-user scoping cosmetic. When User A renders /account?tab=billing, their billingAddress and cardLast4 are stored under '/account?tab=billing'; when User B (or any caller, including an admin tool or pre-renderer) requests the identical path+query, they receive User A's page silently, with no error, log, or fallback. This is the same underlying defect described five different ways across the critic findings (missing identity in key, missing ownership check on hit, unused userId, any-caller reachability) — one bug, one entry.
findings.consequence: A user who opens their account page can be shown another user's billing address and last-4 card digits simply because that other user visited the same URL first.

findings.description: The cache is a process-lifetime singleton Map with no TTL, eviction, or invalidation, so any cached PII persists indefinitely.
findings.reasoning: cache.ts declares a module-scope Map with no expiry or invalidation. On its own this is not the isolation bug — it does not cause the cross-user leak — but it broadens the window of the primary defect: once a user's PII is stored under a shared key, it is served to every subsequent requester of that key for the entire process lifetime, and stale data is never refreshed even after the underlying account changes. Absent the identity-keying bug, this is a durability/staleness concern rather than a disclosure one, so it caps below the primary finding. It is reachable (any cache write persists) but its standalone harm is limited to staleness/unbounded growth.
findings.consequence: Once account data is cached it is never expired or invalidated, so users can keep receiving outdated card/address data and the cache grows without bound for as long as the server runs.

verifications.note: The code directly confirms the finding. cacheKey() takes only path and query — userId is never incorporated. In renderAccountPage(), the cache hit branch returns immediately with no ownership check (`if (hit) return hit;`), meaning any user requesting the same path+query receives whatever cached page was stored first, which contains another user's billingAddress and cardLast4 (PII). The userId is only used on the cache-miss branch to populate the page object. The comment in the source even acknowledges this: '// may be ANOTHER user's account page'.

evidence.quotedCode: export function setCached(key: string, value: unknown) { cache.set(key, value); }

verifications.note: The cache is a module-scope Map with no TTL, eviction, size limit, or invalidation mechanism. Once an entry is written via setCached(), it persists for the entire process lifetime. Combined with the identity-absent key bug, this means another user's PII (billingAddress, cardLast4) stored under a path+query key will be returned to any requester of that same path indefinitely, and will never be refreshed even after the account changes. The finding is accurate: the singleton Map has no expiry logic anywhere in the seam source.
